coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of yellow-emissive carbon dots with a large Stokes shift and their application in fluorimetric imaging of intracellular pH
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shengrui
Ji, Xiaohui
Liu, Jin
Wang, Qin
Jin, Lingxia
coredata.dc:description: A new nanoprobe based on yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) was developed for sensing full-range intracellular pH values. By using o-phenylenediamine as the raw material, Y-CDs with a quantum yield of 31% were prepared through a one-pot solvothermal carbonization method. The Y-CDs exhibited a distinctive fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm with excitation at 450 nm, showing a very large Stokes shift (120 nm). Notably, the nanoprobe revealed a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH value within the range of pH 4.0 to 8.2, exhibiting the ability of this probe to monitor full-range intracellular pH variations. In addition, the nanosensor possessed excellent photostability and fluorescence reversibility in pH measurements and showed excellent selective detection of the influences of other biological species. The CD-based nanoprobe was successfully used to perform quantitative fluorescence imaging of intracellular pH variation, demonstrating its promise for application in cellular systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoprobes
o-Phenylenediamine
Intracellular pH variation
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of potential interfering substances in different pH solutions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of Y-CDs.

The structure characterization of Y-CDs. (A) XPS survey spectrum; high-resolution XPS survey scans of (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, and (D) O 1s spectra of Y-CDs; (E) FT-IR spectrum of Y-CDs; (F) absorption (Abs) and fluorescence emission spectra (Em) of Y-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. Each band was deconvoluted following the literature [12,31–36].

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Y-CDs (20 μg/mL) under various pH values (3.0–11.0); (B) pH-dependent fluorescence intensity changes of Y-CDs; (C) photograph of Y-CDs at different pH levels under 365 nm UV light. λex/λem = 450/570 nm.

Fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs in the presence of interfering substances in 20 mM buffer at different pH levels. (λex/λem = 450/570 nm). 1, Blank; 2, Na+; 3, K+; 4, Ca2+; 5, Mg2+; 6, Cu2+; 7, Zn2+; 8, Al3+; 9, Ba2+; 10, Ni2+; 11, Mn2+; 12, Pb2+; 13, Hg2+; 14, Fe3+; 15, Ag+; 16, Val; 17, Leu; 18, Trp; 19, Ala; 20, Ser; 21, Thr; 22, Arg; 23, Pro (the concentration of 1–23 was 1 mM); 24, GSH; 25, Hcy; 26, Cys; 27, H2O2; and 28, HClO (the concentration of 24–28 was 0.5 mM). (A) pH 5.0; (B) pH 6.0; (C) pH 7.4; (D) pH 8.2.

Confocal fluorescence images of 40 μg/mL Y-CDs incubated with SMMC-7721 cells in high-K+ HEPES-buffered solution in the presence of nigericin at different pH levels. Excitation wavelength for Y-CDs: 458 nm; emission collection: 475–575 nm. Scale bar: 20 μm.
Confocal microscope analysis of SMMC-7721 cells treated with CQ and DEX. Excitation wavelength for Y-CDs: 458 nm; emission collection: 475–575 nm. Scale bar: 20 μm.
Schematic illustration of one-step synthesis of Y-CDs.
A comparison of CD-based methods for detection of pH value.

row.entry: Real-time imaging of intracellular pH value

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of yellow-emissive carbon dots with a large Stokes shift and their application in fluorimetric imaging of intracellular pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi, 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi, 723000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new nanoprobe based on yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) was developed for sensing full-range intracellular pH values. By using o-phenylenediamine as the raw material, Y-CDs with a quantum yield of 31% were prepared through a one-pot solvothermal carbonization method. The Y-CDs exhibited a distinctive fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm with excitation at 450 nm, showing a very large Stokes shift (120 nm). Notably, the nanoprobe revealed a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH value within the range of pH 4.0 to 8.2, exhibiting the ability of this probe to monitor full-range intracellular pH variations. In addition, the nanosensor possessed excellent photostability and fluorescence reversibility in pH measurements and showed excellent selective detection of the influences of other biological species. The CD-based nanoprobe was successfully used to perform quantitative fluorescence imaging of intracellular pH variation, demonstrating its promise for application in cellular systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular pH is heterogeneous across organelles, varying from 4.5 to 5.5 in lysosome to approximate 8.0 in mitochondria [1]. Cellular pH variations are indicative of cell function changes, and abnormal pH may induce dysfunction of the organelles and is a hallmark of many diseases, such as cancer and Alzheimer’s disease [2,3]. Various methods for sensing intracellular pH, such as H+-permeable microelectrode methods and optical microscopy, have been developed [4,5]. Among these various methods, fluorescence-based techniques are attracting attention due to their advantages of noninvasiveness, rapid response, and excellent spatiotemporal resolution [6]. Currently, construction of fluorescent sensors the limiting factor of using fluorescence to image pH values in living cells.

To date, several types of fluorescent probes, such as organic dyes, fluorescent proteins and nanomaterials, have been widely used to detect local pH values inside cells [7–10]. There are several disadvantages to the use of organic dyes and fluorescent proteins, such as tedious procedures, rapid photobleaching, and complicated extraction and purification steps [11,12]. By contrast, nanomaterial-based pH indicators possess high photostability, excellent chemical inertness, and other beneficial properties. To date, pH nanoprobes based on semiconductor quantum dots [13], upconversion nanoparticles [14], silica, gold nanoparticles [15,16], Ag nanoclusters [17,18], and graphene quantum dots [19], have been constructed. However, the use of these nanomaterials has several drawbacks, including complex synthesis procedures, large particle sizes, and a small Stokes shift (Table S1). As an alternative to these nanomaterials, CDs are good scaffolds for pH detection due to their simple manufacturing process, high photostability, favorable biocompatibility, and small particle size, which may facilitate their diffusion into cells [20–22]. However, most CD-based sensors suffer from the general problem of small Stokes shift or short wavelength excitation or emission, limiting their potential application in practical analysis. Therefore, a promising CD-based nanoprobe with a large Stokes shift and long wavelength emission for detecting intracellular pH values is needed.

Metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Ag+), amino acids (Val, Leu, Trp, Ala, Ser, Thr, Arg, Pro), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), HClO, H2O2, o-PD, chloroquine (CQ), dexamethasone (DEX) and nigericin sodium salt were all purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazoyl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98%) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, U.S.A.). All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken with a G2F 2.0 FEI EIectron optics instrument (Thermo Scientific, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi multitechnique surface analyzer (Thermo Scientific, America). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum analysis was carried out using a Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescent (PL) lifetime was performed with an Edinburgh Analytical FLS1000 instrument (Techcomp, China). All the fluorescence spectra were recorded on a RF5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the images were acquired on an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser scanning microscope (Olympus, Japan).

Briefly, 100 mg of o-PD was dissolved in 10 mL of C2H5OH, and 40 μL of concentrated HNO3 was added under vigorous stirring. Then, the solution was transferred into a 25 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained dark brown suspension was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to discard the nonfluorescent precipitate, and the remaining solution was purified via silica column chromatography using a mixture of methylene dichloride and methanol as the eluent (CH2Cl2/CH3OH, 8: 1 v/v). After evaporating the solvent and further drying under vacuum, the Y-CD sample was obtained.

The QY of the nanosensor was measured according to eq (1):
(1)
∅
x
=
∅
s t
(
D
x
D
s t
)
(
A
s t
A
x
)
(
n
x
2
n
s t
2
)
where Φ
st is the QY of the standard, D is the area under the emission spectra, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and n is the refractive index of the solvent used. x subscript denotes unknown, and st means standard. Rhodamine B (Φf = 0.66 in ethanol) was selected as the standard.

A total of 10 mg of Y-CDs was dissolved in 10 mL of C2H5OH to obtain the stock solution. In a set of test tubes, 100 μL of Y-CDs suspension (1.0 mg/mL) was added to 4.90 mL of buffer at one of several pH values. The resulting solution was well mixed, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. The reversibility of the Y-CDs suspension was investigated by adjusting the pH values between 4.0 and 8.2 with HCl and NaOH solutions, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The fluorescence emission intensity of the nanoprobe was measured in buffer at different pH values. The pH profiles of fluorescence intensity were fitted to the Henderson-Hasselbach equation for the determination of pK a [26,27]:
(2)
p
K
a
= p H − l o g
I
m a x
− I
I −
I
m i n
where I is the fluorescence intensity of the testing compound at a given wavelength, I max and I min are the maximum and minimum limiting values of I, respectively, and K a is the acid dissociation constant.

SMMC-7721 cells were seeded in 96-well cell culture plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h before treatment. Afterward, the cells were cultured with different concentrations of Y-CDs (0–60 μg/mL) for 12 h. The cells were then treated in medium containing 0.5 mg/mL of MTT for 4 h. Finally, the supernatant was replaced by 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution, and the absorbance of MTT at 562 nm was measured using a microplate reader (WD-2102A Microplate Reader, Beijing Liuyi Instruments, Inc.). All MTT assays were performed four times in duplicate.

SMMC-7721 cells were seeded in culture plates containing sterile coverslips and allowed to adhere for 24 h prior to treatment. The cells were exposed to Y-CDs (40 μg/mL) for 4 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) three times and treated with 5 μg/mL of nigericin in PBS for another 10 min. After that, the Y-CD-loaded cells were incubated with high-concentration K+ buffer at one of various pH values (4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.4 or 8.2) for 30 min. Cell imaging was then performed using an Olympus FV1000 laser scanning confocal microscope. The excitation wavelength for the Y-CDs was 458 nm, and the emission collection was 475–575 nm. For the drug-stimuli experiment, SMMC-7721 cells were first treated with 200 μM of chloroquine (CQ) for 1 h and 1 μM dexamethasone (DEX) for 6 h (with CQ added to increase lysosomal pH, and DEX used to induce cellular acidification and apoptosis) [28,29]. The medium was removed, and the cells were subsequently incubated with 40 μg/mL Y-CDs for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, images were obtained using the procedures described above.

Analysis of the morphology and the internal characterization of Y-CDs were performed by using TEM and HRTEM. The TEM image of Y-CDs (Fig. 1 ) shows that the Y-CDs were spherical in shape and showed excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution. The corresponding Gaussian fitting size distribution curve showed that the Y-CDs exhibit a narrow size distribution, with an average size of 2.4 nm (Fig. S1). The internal structural characterization of Y-CDs performed by HRTEM image analysis revealed that the Y-CDs have clear lattice structures with a fringe spacing of 0.24 nm (inset of Fig. 1), corresponding to the basal spacing of (1120) the lattice plane of graphene [30].

The elemental compositions and complex surface functionalities were identified by XPS and FT-IR spectra analysis. The full-range XPS spectrum revealed three typical peaks at 285.08, 399.04 and 532.03 eV, which were associated with the presence of C 1s (94.92%), N 1s (2.97%) and O 1s (2.11%) (Fig. 2 A). In the high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2B–D), the high-resolution C 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 285.08, 287.33, and 289.18 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and O–CO bonds, respectively [12,31]. The deconvolution of the N 1s spectrum showed four peaks at 396.43, 398.48, 399.97, and 403.38 eV, representing –CN [12], pyridinic N [32], amino N [33] and nitro N bonds [34], which may have derived from the HNO3 added in the reaction mixture. This result suggests that HNO3 can reacted with o-PD through electrophilic reaction and thereby affect the structure of Y-CDs via oxidation, protonation, polymerization, and carbonization reactions [35]. The O 1s deconvolution showed oxygenated carbon atoms (CO/C–OH) at 531.83 eV [36]. Subsequently, FT-IR characterization was performed to confirm the chemical bonds on the surface of the Y-CDs. Fig. 2E depicts the FT-IR spectrum of the Y-CDs and shows that the Y-CDs possess –NH2 (3375 and 3173 cm−1), aliphatic CH2 (2921 cm−1), OC–O– (1821 cm-1), CO/CN (1641 cm−1), –NO2 (1489 cm−1), CC (1420 cm−1), C–C (1223 cm−1), C–O (1130 cm−1) and C–H (845 and 748 cm−1) functional groups or chemical bonds. Taken together, the results of the XPS and FT-IR analysis confirm that the Y-CDs were mainly composed of C, O, N elements and that –NH2 and –COOH groups are present on the surface of the Y- CDs.

The optical behaviors of Y-CDs were analyzed by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2F, the UV–vis spectrum of the Y-CDs in aqueous solution showed four different absorption bands at approximately 246, 267, 385, and 430 nm, respectively. The absorption bands at 246 and 267 nm correspond to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bond [37]. The absorbance peaks at 385 and 430 nm correspond to n−π* transition, which may be attributed to the existence of CO or CN associated with the graphitic framework of Y-CDs [38]. In contrast, the emission wavelength of the Y-CDs showed nearly no shift with the change of excitation wavelength over the range of 370–510 nm with an interval of 20 nm, revealing the excitation-independent fluorescence behavior of the Y-CDs. The maximal excitation and emission wavelengths of the Y-CDs were 450 and 570 nm, showing a large Stokes shift (120 nm). In addition, the excitation spectrum of the Y-CDs exhibited extensive overlap with their absorption spectrum in the lower-energy region (Fig. S2), indicating that the luminescent centers of the Y-CDs were consistent with their absorption structures [12]. Using Rhodamine B (Φ f = 0.66 in ethanol) as a fluorescence standard, the QY of the Y-CDs in aqueous solution was calculated to be 31%.

The influence of the concentration of the obtained Y-CDs on their fluorescence intensity was investigated (Fig. S3). As the concentration of the Y-CD solution increased from 5 to 40 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm initially increased before reaching its maximal value at 20 μg/mL. Thus, the concentration of the Y-CD solution was chosen to be 20 μg/mL. Subsequently, standard fluorescence pH titrations were performed in 20 mM buffer with different pH values. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, as the pH was increased from 3.0 to 11.0, the fluorescence emission intensity at 570 nm gradually increased, ultimately increasing by 41.4-fold; this increase was accompanied by a pronounced color change as observed under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3C).

To explore the mechanism underlying the response to pH, TEM images of Y-CDs at different pH values were obtained (Fig. S4). As expected, the average sizes of the Y-CDs at pH 4.0 and 8.2 were 2.5 and 2.1 nm, respectively, indicating that no aggregation of the Y-CDs occurred with increasing pH. Therefore, we inferred that the fluorescence enhancement of the Y-CDs at high pH values was not attributable to aggregation. Subsequently, fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed. As shown in Fig. S5 and Table S3, the PL decays of the Y-CD solution were bi-exponential, and the average lifetime increased from 5.54 to 20.39 ns, indicating that the nonradiative transition rate decreased as the pH increased from 4.0 to 8.2 [39]. Therefore, we speculated that the enhanced fluorescent intensity may be attributed to the negatively charged protective shell derived from carboxylates on the surface of the Y-CDs, which limited the nonradiative recombination rate of the photoexcited state of the Y-CDs to produce marked fluorescence enhancement [40]. Fig. 3B shows that the sigmoidal fitting of pH-dependent fluorescence at 570 nm yielded a pK a value of 6.4, matching the detection of intracellular pH values. In particular, it was confirmed that the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm was linear against pH variation in the range of 4.0–8.2, according to the linear regression equation F 570nm = 76.88pH–302.14, with a linear coefficient of 0.9979 (Fig. S6). The linear range was wide and meets the requirements for in vivo imaging and sensing of intracellular pH. These results demonstrated that Y-CDs can be applied as a new kind of nanoprobe for the quantitative determination of pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of potential interfering substances in different pH solutions
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the Y-CDs as a nanoprobe for pH sensing in complex intracellular environments, the potential interference of metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Ag+), amino acids (Val, Leu, Trp, Ala, Ser, Thr, Arg, Pro), biothiols (GSH, Cys and Hcy) and reactive oxygen species (H2O2, HClO) were investigated at pH 5.0, 6.0, 7.4 and 8.2. As shown in Fig. 4 , no obvious variation in fluorescence intensity of the Y-CDs was observed in the presence of these potentially interfering substances, demonstrating the excellent selectivity of this nanoprobe.

The photostability of this nanoprobe was tested by measuring the fluorescent response in 20 mM buffer with different pH values (6.0 and 8.2) for 1 h. Fig. S7A shows that a negligible change in fluorescent emission intensity was apparent during the scanning process at room temperature, indicating that this nanoprobe is superior to organic probes in terms of stability upon long-duration light irradiation and exposure to air. Next, the reversible response against pH was investigated by repeatedly alternating the pH value between 4.0 and 8.2 by using HCl and NaOH as modulators. As shown in Fig. S7B, these processes presented good reproducibility after repetition for four continual cycles. Taken together, the results indicate that the CD-based nanosensor exhibited good photostability and fluorescence reversibility in different pH solutions.

Before applying the nanosensor for the imaging of intracellular pH, a standard MTT assay was performed to investigate the cytotoxicity of the Y-CDs. As shown in Fig. S8, SMMC-7721 cells maintained more than 90% of their initial viability, even at nanosensor concentrations up to 60 μg/mL, indicating that the nanosensor has minimal cytotoxicity. To investigate the nanosensor’s performance in cell imaging, SMMC-7721 cells were first incubated with Y-CDs (40 μg/mL) for 4 h at 37 °C. Then, the cells were treated with buffer at pH 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.4 or 8.2 for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 5 , the yellow fluorescence intensity gradually increased as the pH increased from 4.0 to 8.2. The average fluorescence intensities were determined and exhibited pH dependence, with strong linearity over the pH range from 4.0 to 8.2 in living cells (Fig. S9). This linear correlation reveals that the Y-CDs can allow the precise quantitative measurement of pH values in intracellular microenvironments with different pH ranges.

Encouraged by the above-mentioned outcome, we applied the Y-CDs to sense intracellular pH variations induced by different drugs to evaluate whether this nanosensor is capable of real-time sensing of intracellular pH fluctuations under abnormal circumstances (Fig. 6 ). Compared with the images of control cells, the images of SMMC-7721 cells stimulated with CQ showed a stronger fluorescence intensity, and those of cells exposed to DEX exhibited a weaker fluorescence intensity. These satisfying intracellular results revealed that the Y-CDs can not only quantitatively and efficiently sense full-range intracellular pH values but also can allow the real-time detection of pH values of cells exposed to various external stimuli.

We compared the features of some published CD-based pH sensors to the Y-CDs [40–49]. As presented in Table 1 , this nanosensor has a larger Stokes shift (120 nm) and a longer wavelength emission (570 nm) than those of previous CD-based pH sensors, making this nanosensor comparable to previous sensors. Due to its unique emission properties, this nanoprobe can be applied not only to measure normal cellular pH values but also to perform real-time monitoring of stimulus-response changes of intracellular pH. The latter function cannot be performed by the other pH sensors listed in Table 1. The single-wavelength emission is the major limitation of the Y-CDs, which can be addressed by developing CD-based ratiometric nanoprobes, extending the scope of applications involving the sensitive measurement of intracellular pH.

A CD-based fluorescent probe was developed that sensitively and accurately senses full-range intracellular pH values. This nanoprobe possesses a yellow-emissive wavelength (570 nm) and a large Stokes shift (120 nm), features valuable for diminishing the measurement error caused by the excitation light and scattered light, allowing reliable and sensitive in vitro and vivo pH monitoring. The apparent pK a of 6.4, the broad acid/base transition width of ∼4.0 pH units, low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility render the Y-CDs an ideal nanoprobe for the precise quantification of pH values in SMMC-7721 cells. This probe represents an alternative and convenient tool for the analysis of intracellular pH variation and highlights the potential practical applications of CDs in biological systems.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21807068, 2160313) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (Nos. 2019JQ060, 2019JQ006, 2019JQ149), the Educational Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Province of China (19JK0192) and the Young Talent fund of University Association for Science and Technology in Shaanxi, China (20180607).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117677.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent bioimaging of pH: from design to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.T. Hou, W.X. Ren, K. Li, J. Seo, A. Sharma, X.Q. Yu, J.S. Kim, Fluorescent bioimaging of pH: from design to applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 46 (2017) 2076-2090.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-age related differences in thrombin responses by platelets from male patients with advanced Alzheimer’s disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.A. Davies, R.E. Fine, R.J. Johnson, C.A. Levesque, W.H. Rathbun, K.F. Seetoo, S.J. Smith, G. Strohmeier, L. Volicer, L. Delva, Non-age related differences in thrombin responses by platelets from male patients with advanced Alzheimer’s disease, Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 194 (1993) 537-543.
A. Roos, W.F. Boron, Intracellular pH, Physiol. Rev. 61 (1981) 296-434.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intracellular pH sensors: design principles and functional significance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Srivastava, D.L. Barber, M.P. Jacobson, Intracellular pH sensors: design principles and functional significance, Physiology 22 (2007) 30-39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent probes and bioimaging: alkali metals, alkaline earth metals and pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yin, Y. Hu, J. Yoon, Fluorescent probes and bioimaging: alkali metals, alkaline earth metals and pH, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (2014) 4619-4644.
J.Y. Han, K. Burgess, Fluorescent indicators for intracellular pH, Chem. Rev. 110 (2009) 2709-2728.
D. Wencel, T. Abel, C. Mcdonagh, Optical chemical pH sensors, Anal. Chem. 86 (2014) 15-29.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric detection of intracellular lysine and pH with one-pot synthesized dual emissive carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Song, W. Duan, Y. Liu, Z. Ye, Y. Chen, H. Chen, S. Qi, J. Wu, D. Liu, L. Xiao, Ratiometric detection of intracellular lysine and pH with one-pot synthesized dual emissive carbon dots, Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 13626-13633.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of orange fluorescence carbon dots with excitation independent emission for pH sensing and cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yuan, X.J. Gong, H. Han, Y.F. Gao, W.J. Lu, Y. Liu, M. Xian, S.M. Shuang, C. Dong, Facile synthesis of orange fluorescence carbon dots with excitation independent emission for pH sensing and cellular imaging, Anal. Chim. Acta 1042 (2018) 125-132.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free carbon-dots-based ratiometric fluorescence pH nanoprobes for intracellular pH sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Shangguan, D. He, X. He, K. Wang, F. Xu, J. Liu, J. Tang, X. Yang, J. Huang, Label-free carbon-dots-based ratiometric fluorescence pH nanoprobes for intracellular pH sensing, Anal. Chem. 88 (2016) 7837-7843.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of yellow-green-emissive carbon dots and their application in constructing a fluorescent turn-on nanoprobe for imaging of selenol in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, S. Zhang, Y. Zhong, X.F. Yang, Z. Li, H. Li, Preparation of yellow-green-emissive carbon dots and their application in constructing a fluorescent turn-on nanoprobe for imaging of selenol in living cells, Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 1734-1741.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dot-fluorescent protein FRET probes for sensing intracellular pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Dennis, W.J. Rhee, D. Sotto, S.N. Dublin, G. Bao, Quantum dot-fluorescent protein FRET probes for sensing intracellular pH, ACS Nano 6 (2012) 2917-2924.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel “modularized” optical sensor for pH monitoring in biological matrixes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, S.Q. Zhang, X. Wei, T. Yang, M.L. Chen, J.H. Wang, A novel “modularized” optical sensor for pH monitoring in biological matrixes, Biosens. Bioelectron. 109 (2018) 150-155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modified organosilica core-shell nanoparticles for stable pH sensing in biological solutions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.J. Robinson, G.T. Huynh, B.P. Kouskousis, N.L. Fletcher, Z.H. Houston, K.J. Thurecht, S.R. Corrie, Modified organosilica core-shell nanoparticles for stable pH sensing in biological solutions, ACS Sensors 3 (2018) 967-975.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of pH-sensitive “Submarine” based on gold nanoparticles with double insurance for intracellular pH mapping, quantifying of whole cells and in vivo applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.K. Yu, K. Li, H.H. Qin, Q. Zhou, C.H. Qian, Y.H. Liu, X.Q. Yu, Construction of pH-sensitive “Submarine” based on gold nanoparticles with double insurance for intracellular pH mapping, quantifying of whole cells and in vivo applications, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 22839-22848.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent pH sensor based on Ag@SiO2 core-shell nanoparticle
